title: Median income or consumption per day vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption: Median income or consumption per day rises alongside GDP per capita across countries in 2024, with both axes plotted on logarithmic scales to accommodate wide disparities in wealth. The horizontal axis measures GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, ranging from under $2,000 to over $50,000, while the vertical axis tracks median daily income or consumption from $2 to above $50. Circles represent individual countries, sized by population, and colored by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A clear positive relationship emerges — higher GDP per capita coincides with higher median daily income or consumption. The United States sits at the upper right, with GDP per capita exceeding $50,000 and median daily income or consumption above $50. Japan, Türkiye, and Russia cluster between $20,000 and $50,000 GDP per capita, with median daily values between $20 and $50. China and Iran fall near $10,000–$20,000 GDP per capita, with medians around $10–$20. India, Indonesia, and Pakistan occupy the $5,000–$10,000 GDP range, with medians between $5 and $10. At the lower end, Burundi, DR Congo, Madagascar, Uganda, Ethiopia, Zambia, Kenya, Nigeria, Gambia, Comoros, and Kiribali all report GDP per capita below $5,000 and median daily income or consumption under $5. Palestine and Nepal appear slightly above this group, with GDP near $5,000 and medians approaching $10. Georgia and Kazakhstan sit between $10,000 and $20,000 GDP, with medians around $10–$20. Serbia and Vietnam lie just below $10,000 GDP, with medians near $10. The largest circles — India, China, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, United States — reflect their populations, yet their positions follow the same upward-sloping pattern as smaller nations. The data shows no country with high GDP per capita and low median daily income or consumption, nor any with low GDP and high median daily value. The dominant pattern is a consistent upward slope: as GDP per capita increases, median daily income or consumption increases in tandem.
